coredata.dc:title: Chameleon photonic crystals: Acid-induced emission and absorption shifts for sensor applications

coredata.dc:creator: Zając, Weronika
Czajkowski, Maciej
Cybińska, Joanna
coredata.dc:description: Thanks to their optical properties, photonic crystals are a very interesting group of modern materials. Their applicability is further enhanced by the possibility of doping with stimuli-responsive compounds. This paper presents a preparation and characterization of photonic crystals doped with a luminescent dye – ethyl eosin. By doping with this dye, a material with sensor properties and unique optical properties such as angle-dependent emission was obtained. The study focused on determining the luminescence properties of the opals and the dye, specifically their quantum yield (QY). Moreover, the obtained dye-doped photonic crystals exhibit a rapid change in luminescence properties and color after contact with vapors of inorganic acids commonly used in industry. Furthermore, a three-dimensional opal doped with ethyl eosin exhibits a selective change in the presence of nitric acid(V), unlike the dye solution. These luminescent and sensing properties, coupled with reversibility, make this material fascinating.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photonic crystal
Sensor properties ethyl eosin
Luminescent dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General experimental information on synthesis and characterization
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis of the spheres
Determination of the size of the spheres forming 3D opals by optical Bragg reflection measurements
Characterization of absorption and photoluminescence properties
Absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the dyes and dye-doped spheres
Measurements of angle-dependent emission color of the 3D opals

LIDER/39/0203/L-12/20/NCBR/2021.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determined asymmetric unit of the ethyl eosin crystal obtained.
SEM images and histograms of the size distribution of the synthesized dye-doped PMMA spheres.

a) Normalized emission (pink line) and absorption (blue line) spectra of the studied dye dissolved in ethanol, b) Normalized emission spectrum of the dye in a solid (pink line) and normalized absorption spectrum of PC1 sample (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Normalized emission spectra of the synthesized PC2 opal with respect to the angle. The inset shows part of the chromaticity diagram of the emission.

a) Dependence of QY and spheres' average diameter on the weight fraction of the dye in the EE-doped PMMA opals. QY values of EE in solid and in EtOH are indicated by dashed lines. b) Normalized emission spectra of the PC5 sample before and after the contact with selected acid vapors. The photographs in the inset demonstrate the color changes of the material when exposed to acid vapors. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

a) Photographs of reversible color changes observed in a powdered sample of PC5 in consecutive cycles when alternately exposed to acids vapor and saturated KOH solution, b) Prototropic equilibria for ethyl eosin. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The reflectance spectra of the 3D opal sample PC5 recorded at various incidence angles before and after acid vapor treatment.

head.ce:title: Chameleon photonic crystals: Acid-induced emission and absorption shifts for sensor applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Łukasiewicz Research Network–PORT Polish Center for Technology Development

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thanks to their optical properties, photonic crystals are a very interesting group of modern materials. Their applicability is further enhanced by the possibility of doping with stimuli-responsive compounds. This paper presents a preparation and characterization of photonic crystals doped with a luminescent dye – ethyl eosin. By doping with this dye, a material with sensor properties and unique optical properties such as angle-dependent emission was obtained. The study focused on determining the luminescence properties of the opals and the dye, specifically their quantum yield (QY). Moreover, the obtained dye-doped photonic crystals exhibit a rapid change in luminescence properties and color after contact with vapors of inorganic acids commonly used in industry. Furthermore, a three-dimensional opal doped with ethyl eosin exhibits a selective change in the presence of nitric acid(V), unlike the dye solution. These luminescent and sensing properties, coupled with reversibility, make this material fascinating.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial growth necessitates the development of efficient and cost-effective technologies fordetecting various kinds of contaminants to prevent their negative impact on human health and the environment. However, one of the current challenges is a straightforward identification of concentrated acids such as sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and nitric acid (HNO3), which are essential in various industries [1–3] and pose a serious health hazard if mishandled.

The answer to this demand could be the use of photonic crystals (PCs) as a detection and analysis medium. These materials exhibit a spatially periodic distribution of refractive index [4] in one, two, or three dimensions resulting in such effects like diffraction and selective reflection (Bragg reflection) of light [5]. Furthermore, the literature reveals potential methods of doping PCs with active components such as luminescent dyes [6,7], quantum dots [8], or lanthanide nanoparticles [9]. PCs can manipulate the incident light but also the light coming from their luminescent dopants, resulting in a dependence of the observed emission color on the viewing angle [10]. However, in the case of this so-called angle-dependent emission, it's difficult to compare the magnitude of the changes basing only on the emission spectra. A simple characterization parameter is searched for.

In the case of doping PCs with luminescent dyes, the form of the dye in the opal plays an important role. It is because, many organic luminescent dyes exhibit high quantum yield (QY) in the separated state, e.g. in diluted solutions, and low QY in the solid state [11], due to aggregation. The literature usually does not indicate the form in which luminescent dyes are present in the opals, although this property can have a significant impact on the QY of the materials obtained. Additionally, it is possible to appropriately modify PCs so that they exhibit a change in properties, e.g. optical (emission, absorption), in the presence of specific analytes such as ions [12,13], gases [14,15] or chemical compounds [16,17]. PCs can also be used to study changes in: pH [18], temperature [19], or humidity [20]. The use of PCs in sensors provides additional benefits in terms of their optical properties, including the ability to detect changes in the refractive index of the surrounding medium. Additional advantages may include sensitivity, stability, miniaturization, and real-time testing capability. Colloidal PCs appear to be an ideal material for vapor detection, because of their large specific surface area for absorbing analytes [21].

This paper presents the possibilities of using poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) PCs as selective sensors of acid vapors. The properties of the PCs were obtained through doping with a highly luminescent dye – ethyl eosin (EE). The aim was to obtain a dye-doped 3D opal that would exhibit a rapid visual change in the optical properties upon exposure to acid vapors. In addition, the goal was also to obtain and fully characterize the material with unique optical properties (such as the angle-dependent emission) and to determine, how the concentration of the dye affects the QY of the opals. A high QY value could be critical for improving the performance and reliability of sensors that rely on luminescence changes to detect various substances. It should be mentioned that although the sodium salt of EE is known and used in histopathology for tissue staining, the crystal structure and some optical properties of EE, such as the QY value, have not yet been determined.

2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionamidine) dihydrochloride (AAPH, 97 %) and fluorescein (98 %) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich. Methyl methacrylate (MMA, 98 %) was purchased from Acros Organics. Bromine (98.5 %) was purchased from Chempur. All the reagents were used without further purification. Deionized water with conductivity not higher than 0.1
μ S
c m
from a water deionizer HLP 5UV (Hydrolab) was used. All organic solvents were purchased from commercial sources and used without prior purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General experimental information on synthesis and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the EE dye synthesis and PCs preparation can be found in SI. The nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H and 13C) were recorded on an Avance III HD 600 MHz (Bruker) NMR spectrometer. The High-Resolution Electrospray Ionization Mass Spectroscopy (ESI-HRMS) spectra were recorded on an ESI-Q-TOF Maxis Impact Mass Spectrometer (Bruker).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis of the spheres
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of the synthesized spheres, as well as their size, were determined by SEM. For this purpose, a SEM – Helios NanoLab 660 (FEI) – was used. Depending on the sample the diameter measurements were performed on a number of 150–250 individual spheres. The polydispersity of the sphere diameter was characterized by statistical parameters such as standard deviation (σ) and relative standard deviation (RSD), given by equation (1):
(1)
R S D =
σ
D ‾
× 100
[ % ]
where:
D ‾
– is an average sphere diameter [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the size of the spheres forming 3D opals by optical Bragg reflection measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Measurements of the position of the optical Bragg reflection band of the synthesized 3D opals in dependence on the incidence angle were performed in a goniospectrophotometer setup, based on optomechanical system 8–0045.4 (Standa), equipped with a light source and a detector mounted on two independent rotating arms having the same rotation axis. A CCD camera – QE65 Pro (Ocean Optics) equipped with an optical fiber was used as the detector. A halogen lamp OSL2 Fiber Illuminator (Thor Labs) emitting light in a spectral range from 350 to 1200 nm was used as the light source. The system consisted also of a height-adjustable stage, where the samples of the 3D opals were placed. Nearly plane-parallel samples of the fabricated 3D opals were placed with the position of their top adjusted to the axis of rotation of the arms. Thus, the plane (111) of the colloidal crystal was in the plane of the sample. During the measurements, the angular positions of the detector and the light source were changed within a range of 20°–70°, symmetrically, with respect to the normal to the sample, and the spectra were collected. A distinct spectral band shifting with the angle of the incidence indicated the Bragg reflection. The measurements were repeated several times on different sample positions in order to determine the uncertainty of the diameter of the spheres measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of absorption and photoluminescence properties
Absorption and photoluminescence spectra of the dyes and dye-doped spheres
ce:section.ce:para: In the first step, a series of solutions of the studied dye in ethanol was prepared. The measurements of UV–Vis absorption spectra of the dye solutions were recorded in a transmission setup on the Evolution 300 (Thermo Fisher) spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra of the resulting photonic crystals were measured using the diffuse reflectance accessory – Praying Mantis (Harrick). Then, measurements of the emission and the excitation spectra of the dye solutions and dye-doped spheres were carried out using FLS980 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a xenon lamp Xe1 (Edinburgh Instruments). The measurements were performed in quartz cuvettes.

QY of the dye-doped spheres and the studied dye in the solid state were measured in the Integrating Sphere Assembly F-M01 (Edinburgh Instruments) dedicated to the FLS980 spectrofluorimeter. The equipment manufacturer specified the estimated uncertainty of the absolute QY determination at the level of 5 % and repeatability uncertainty at the level of 3 % for standard measurement procedure. For the QY measurements, the dye-doped PMMA spheres were ground in a mortar with undoped PMMA spheres. The ground materials were used for the preparation of pastilles using a hydraulic press – Mini-Pellet Press (Specac). For the QY measurements, the samples in the form of tablets were placed in a cup-shaped holder designed for solid-state samples. A reference sample was a pastille made of BaSO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of angle-dependent emission color of the 3D opals
ce:section.ce:para: Measurements of the emission spectra of the fabricated 3D dye-doped PMMA opals as a function of the viewing angle were performed in a goniospectrophotometer setup modified with respect to that setup used for optical Bragg reflection measurements. CCD camera – QE65 Pro (Ocean Optics) equipped with an optical fiber was used as a detector and it was mounted on the rotating arm. The sample was put on a glass substrate on a height-adjustable stage so that the top of the sample was adjusted to the axis of rotation of the detector's arm. Excitation of the EE samples was performed by NovaPro diode laser (RGB Lasersystems GmbH), emitting light of output power of 75 mW at λ = 405 nm, equipped with a gray gradient filter for setting the incident light intensity. 3D opal samples with a thickness in the range from 2 to 5 mm were excited from the below at the incident angle of around 45°. For the measurement, the detector was collecting the emitted light from the top side of the sample at different angles. The detection angle with respect to the normal to the sample plane was set in a range from 0° to 55° and the emission spectrum was recorded. To assess measurement uncertainty, angle-dependent emission measurements were repeated three times on different parts of the samples.

The first step toward the preparation of the dye-doped spheres was to choose and synthesize the dye. Details of the synthesis are described in the Experimental section in SI. It should be mentioned that a synthesis route to obtain EE was already present in the literature [23]. However, with our modified method, the compound was obtained with much higher efficiency (60.9 % vs 35 %). The structure of the compound was confirmed by 1H NMR (Fig. S1),13C NMR (Fig. S2), and mass spectrometry studies. The preparation pathway allowed to obtain a monocrystal of the dye, which was used to determine its crystallographic structure, for the first time. The characterization of the crystallographic arrangement allows a better understanding of the material features, which is important in the case of obtaining modern materials with well-defined properties.

An asymmetric unit of the crystal (Fig. 1 ) contains one molecule of EE, one molecule of water, and two molecules of THF. Red block-like crystals of EE were indexed with unit cell parameters of a = 11.286(2), b = 11.634(2), c = 13.344(3), α = 79.72(2), β = 69.91(2), γ = 67.15(2) of triclinic space group P
1 ‾
. For details on the structure determination methodology based on references [24–27] and more information about monocrystalline structure of EE and, see SI. Moreover, CIF files are deposited in the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre (CCDC (2310819).

Crystallographic analysis suggests that the molecules in the crystal are arranged “head-to-tail”. This observation may indicate the creation of J-aggregates [28]. An intriguing observation reveals intermolecular interactions between the oxygen atom and the nearest bromine atom of the dye. The O5⋯Br1 distance in the crystal is found to be equal to 3.03 Å. This value is smaller than the sum of the van der Waals radii of oxygen and bromine (3.35 Å [29]). This value may be interesting because non-covalent interactions are still the subject of extensive research.

The next step was to synthesize PCs doped with EE. The synthesis of poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) PCs was carried out according to a modified method with respect to that described in the literature [30]. For the comparison in the first phase of the study, two syntheses of PMMA spheres were performed which differed in the amount of a monomer used, while the percentage of EE (relative to monomer weight) remained consistent at 0.5 % (see Table S1 in SI). The dye-doped PCs were named PC1 (12.5 mL of the monomer) and PC2 (10 mL of the monomer). The corresponding macroscopic photographs of the spheres dispersed in water and the obtained photonic crystal flakes are shown in Fig. S3.

The size of the resulting spheres was determined by SEM and measurement of the position of the Bragg reflection band on specular reflection spectra, recorded in dependence of the incidence angle. The analysis of the results of the optical method was performed according to Ref. [30]. The results of the SEM measurements confirmed that the synthesized particles have a spherical shape (Fig. 2 ). The results of the spheres size measurements by the position of the Bragg reflection band (Fig. S4) correlate well with those obtained by the SEM method (see Table S2 in SI), indicating close-packing of the spheres in the synthesized 3D opals. Furthermore, repeated optical measurements allowed the standard deviation of the diameter to be determined, which was found to be 6 nm for PC1 and 4 nm for PC2, which is relatively low. Histograms (Fig. 2) show the distribution of the sphere's diameter. In the case of each histogram, a dominant size is observed (355 nm and 300 nm), but a small number of much larger spheres also occur. According to the literature, samples with RSD <20 % [30] are considered to be monodisperse. The statistical analysis revealed that doping the spheres during the syntheses yielded spheres that met the established size distribution criteria.

The absorption and emission spectra of the EE dissolved in ethanol (EtOH) are shown in Fig. 3 a. The excitation spectrum (not shown here) corresponds to the absorption spectrum. The Stokes shift value equates to only 595 cm−1.

The emission spectrum of the dye in a solid state was compared with the emission spectrum of the dye dissolved in EtOH (Fig. 3b). For the first, a shift of the emission band maximum towards a longer wavelength and a broadening of the emission band is observed. The literature suggests that the formation of dye aggregates is the underlying cause [31]. However, reabsorption of the light may be another reason. Furthermore, Fig. 3b shows the absorption spectrum of the PC1 sample. The spectrum indicates a significant signal of the shoulder of the band with a maximum of around 500 nm, most probably related to the aggregated form.

in EtOH was found to be nearly the same as that of eosin Y [32,33]. However, it is important to note that the QY of eosin Y in an aqueous solution is only 23 % [34]. The determined spectroscopic properties, including QY and the positions of the absorption and emission band maxima of the dye, are summarized in SI (Table S3).

One of the goals was to optimize the diameter of the spheres to achieve the angle-dependent emission effect of the dye-doped 3D PMMA opals. To achieve this particular effect, the emission range of the incorporated dye must coincide with the reflection range of the Bragg band of the PCs. This makes it possible to observe a continuous change in the shape of the emission spectrum as the viewing angle changes.

As a result of aligning the range of the PC reflection band with the EE emission range, the PC2 opal exhibits angle-dependent emission (Fig. 4 ). To compare the magnitude of emission color changes that occur, the results can be visualized on a chromaticity diagram. The chromaticity coordinates of the emission of the dye-doped 3D opals, which have been optimized in size with respect to the viewing angle, is presented in the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram for the observer at 2°. The most significant part of the diagram is illustrated in the inset in Fig. 4. The complete CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram is shown in Fig. S5.

The diagram shows that for the opal containing EE, the emission color changes from red to orange as the viewing angle increases in the range of 0–55°. To our best knowledge, chromaticity diagrams with color coordinates for the emission of luminescent dye-doped opals have not been previously presented earlier in the literature. The use of a chromaticity diagram makes it possible to easily characterize the materials in terms of color changes of the emission in dependence on the viewing angle.

Moreover, to evaluate reproducibility of the sample preparation, the emission spectra of the synthesized PC2 opal with respect to the viewing angle were measured for three different positions on the sample (Fig. S6). It was observed that the form of the sample and its thickness exert a significant influence on the angle-dependent emission. The samples with the thickness of less than 0.5 cm (marked in blue in the spectra) exhibited less pronounced changes in the emission band shape. Nevertheless, further tests are required to confirm this observation, including the use of other dyes as dopants. The average standard deviation of the position of the band maximum is 3.6 nm for 0°, 6.0 nm for 15°, 12.6 nm for 35°, and 7.1 nm for 55°, respectively.

After optimizing the diameter size, syntheses were performed with different dye content in relation to the amount of the monomer. We decided to conduct the additional syntheses, because of the possibility that the dye might be present in PC2 in an aggregated form, resulting in a low QY. Detailed parameters for the syntheses of the PCs with smaller EE content are presented in SI (Table S1). The weight ratio of EE to the monomer (wt%) were respectively: 1.0 % (PC3), 0.2 % (PC4), 0.05 % (PC5) and 0.02 % (PC6). The corresponding macroscopic photographs of the spheres dispersed in water and the obtained PC flakes are shown in Fig. S3. In the photographs, the color change due to the lower concentration of the dye used and some optical effects due to the properties of the PCs can be observed. As previously, the size of the resulting spheres was determined by SEM and measurement of the position of the Bragg reflection band. Example results are presented in Figs. S7 and S8, respectively. In the case of optical measurements, the procedure was repeated four times for each sample. This allowed the determination of the uncertainty of the spheres diameter measurements. The determined standard deviation in diameter does not exceed 4 % of the average diameter size for all measurements and is summarized in Table S2. It is noteworthy that, despite the similar conditions under which the syntheses were conducted, the resulting sphere sizes differed according to the quantity of the monomer used.

For this reason, we decided to also synthesize undoped PMMA spheres. The synthesis of undoped PC was performed under the same conditions as the PC2 synthesis (Tables S1 and SI), but without EE. The dependence of the mass of EE used in the synthesis on the diameter of the spheres is shown in Fig. 5 a. It can be observed that in the case of syntheses with a small amount of dye (0.05 % and 0.02 %), the average diameter of the spheres is similar to those of the synthesis without the additive and equal to 200 and 220 nm, respectively. Then, as the amount of the dye increases to 0.5 %, the average diameter increases to 300 nm. However, a further increase in the amount of dye did not further increase the diameter. We also observed, that the RSD parameter usually increased with the dye content (see Table S2.). We suppose that dye may also act as a surfactant because only in the case of dye-doped systems was a foaming effect of the mixture observed during the synthesis. The literature indicates, that the use of surfactants in the synthesis of PMMA spheres can cause either a reduction or increase in the spheres’ diameters obtained [35,36]. Further research is necessary to confirm the influence of the dye content on the obtained spheres diameter.

It was also observed that the FWHM of the Bragg bands depends on the polydispersity of the spheres obtained. As an example, for the PC4 sample, with an RSD of around 20 %, the FWHM is 1876 cm−1, while for PC6, with an RSD of around 7 %, the FWHM is only 1272 cm−1. However, it is important to note that the material with the higher RSD still exhibits appropriate reflection properties.

In the case of samples PC3–PC6, due to different diameters of the spheres obtained, the position of the reflection bands does not coincide with the emission band of the dye, and consequently these materials do not exhibit angle-dependent emission. Future research to achieve this phenomenon will require a proper match between the amount of the monomer and the mass of the dye.

The next step to explore the sensor properties of the investigated systems was to measure the QY of the EE-doped opals. For the QY measurements, the dye-doped PMMA spheres were ground in a mortar with undoped PMMA spheres, to reduce the absorbance. The results of the QY measurements are shown in Fig. 5a and summarized in Table S4.

At the outset of the analysis, it is worth noting the large difference in QY between solid EE (3 %) and EE dissolved in EtOH (65 %). EE, as well as many efficient luminescent organic dyes, shows high QY in the separated state, e.g. in solution, and low QY in the solid state, due to aggregation. In the case of the samples containing 1.0 and 0.5 wt% of the dye, the value of the QY is much lower than that of the dye dissolved in EtOH. Most likely, the presence of the dye in an aggregated form reduces the QY. As the dye content in the spheres decreases, the QY increases. Samples possessing 0.05 and 0.02 wt% of the dye have QY close to that of the dye dissolved in EtOH. This indicates that the dye is well dispersed in the matrix and is present in an isolated form. The QY results indicate that the form of the dye in the PMMA spheres varies with its concentration. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study presenting results of absolute QY measurements of dye-doped opals.

The emission spectra of the spheres doped with different quantities of EE were also measured. There is a noticeable shift in the emission band maximum to a longer wavelength as the amount of dye increases. The shift relative to the solution is as follows: 707 cm−1 for 0.05 wt%, 882.5 cm−1 for 0.2 wt% for 1005.4 cm−1 for 1 wt%, and 2038.9 cm−1 for EE in solid. In addition, a broadening of the bands is also noticeable. These observations may indicate a possible aggregation of the EE dye molecules with its increasing concentration, which is supported by the QY results. However, it is also possible that the reabsorption of the light affected the spectra, with the effect also increasing with the dye concentration. The results are presented in SI (Fig. S10).

With the results of the spectroscopic properties of the EE-doped opals, it was possible to begin testing its sensory properties. We have observed that the obtained opals exposed to acids vapor, change both color and luminescent properties. Sulfuric, nitric, and hydrochloric acids were chosen because they are widely used in industry and have a negative impact on human health.

The emission (Fig. 5b) and absorption (Fig. S11) spectra of PC5 were determined after exposure to the three selected acids’ vapors. This sample was chosen because it has a high QY, and is important for the functionality of sensors that rely on changes in luminescence to detect analytes. During the tests, the samples were left in a vessel containing the acid vapors for approximately 10 min.

In cases of absorption and the emission spectra, there are shifts of the bands toward lower wavelengths. A change in the shape of the spectra was also found. The broad absorption bands are shifted as follows: 2100 cm−1 for HCl, 3070 cm−1 for H2SO4, and 6442 cm− 1 for HNO3, respectively, compared to the intact PC5 sample band. The biggest change in both sample color and the emission spectrum is observed in the case of nitric acid. The color change of the samples after contact with the acid vapor is visible to the naked eye. This fact, along with a fast color change, makes the material very versatile. The change in the properties of the materials caused by the presence of the acid vapors is long-lasting. The opals placed in conditions free of the acid vapors remain changed for a duration of up to at least thirty days.

Additionally, the original properties can be restored. The properties can be reversed by immersing the material in a saturated KOH solution. The reversibility of the color of the studied materials after contact with hydrochloric and sulfuric(VI) acid vapors was checked. Fig. 6 a illustrates photographs showing reversible color changes observed in the PC5 sample when exposed to acid vapors, followed by a subsequent transformation upon exposure to KOH solution. The tests were repeated twenty times for both acids, and the photographs were taken under the same lighting conditions. In the case of HCl vapor, no change in the material properties was observed even after twenty cycles. However, with H2SO4, we observed a slight fading of the material after the fifteenth cycle, but the material continued to change color from pink to orange. Additionally, the durability of the resulting material was tested. After six months of air storage the PC5 sample exhibited a reversible color change, demonstrating its durability over time.

To verify that the changes induced by the acids’ vapors were the same, we also measured the emission spectra of the PC2 sample (Fig. S12). The observed changes in both the shape of the bands and the shifts of the bands are similar. To confirm our observations, we also tested the sensory behavior of the dye in a solution. In the first stage of the study, we measured 1H NMR spectra after the addition of deuterosulfuric

acid (D2SO4) (Fig. S13). The spectral analysis shows that there is a visible change in the chemical shifts of the signals. This may indicate the protonation of EE in the acidic medium. The proposition of prototropic equilibria for EE is shown in Fig. 6b. The occurrence of prototropic forms is reported in the literature for compounds structurally similar to EE [37,38]. These tests made it possible to explain the chemical principle of the detection mechanism of the obtained PCs.

The following experiments were conducted to determine if the behavior of the dissolved dye corresponds to that of the dye-doped opal after contact with selected acids. We measured the absorption (Fig. S14) and emission (Fig. S15) spectra for EE dissolved in an ethanol-water mixture. The absorption spectra show a large change due to the addition of acid. There is a noticeable hypsochromic shift at lower pH values compared to the neutral form of EE. This fact also confirms the existence of another prototropic form of the EE dye. The fluorescence spectrum broadens with the addition of acid, and the second band occurs at 585 nm. Analogous band broadening also occurs for fluorescein and eosin Y [39]. In the case of the monoanion form, neither the absorption nor the emission spectra display any significant changes. Moreover, no such high color change due to acid vapor was observed and lack of selectivity for nitric acid was noted for EE in powder form (results not shown here).

The effect of exposition to acid vapors on the position of the photonic crystal band gap was also investigated (Fig. 7 ). A red shift of the reflection band and a decrease in the signal intensity are noticeable in the spectra after contact with acid vapors. The photonic band gap position shifts by 631 cm−1 and 762 cm−1 when the opals are exposed to HCl and HNO3 vapors, respectively. This fact may indicate an inferior order of the spheres. The obtained results allowed us to plot λ
2
max versus sin2 θ dependence, related to a modified form of Bragg's law [40], for the studied samples (Fig. S16). Based on linear fit parameters, it was possible to estimate the positions of the photonic band gap for normal light incidence. The results are summarized in Table S5.

The results demonstrate that EE-doped spheres exhibit greater selectivity towards acid vapor as compared to the dye solution and dye powder. The 3D PCs obtained exhibit a specific change in the case of HNO3. Dye doping at low concentrations resulted in materials displaying greater and more specific changes in spectroscopic properties. However, there is a possibility that the dye may leach out of the PMMA spheres. Moreover, the reaction of the dye is possible only on the surface of the spheres, rather not in their volume. On the other hand, the PCs have a higher surface area than films which makes them good candidates for vapor detection.

There is currently a significant interest in smart, easy-to-use materials due to the need for sensors that respond accurately to changes in the environment in real-time. Recently, a very intriguing area of research has been the potential use of optical sensors that exhibit a rapid, visual change in their properties. Such sensors are known to be effective in determining quantities such as temperature [41,42] and humidity [43]. Nevertheless, one challenge remains: to develop a method for detecting harmful vapors that is independent of sophisticated analytical instruments. The literature is still exploring new solutions for the rapid visual detection of acids’ vapors. To avoid the need for expensive apparatus, colorimetric indicators are being considered [44,45]. Research shows that these materials can detect very low concentrations of acid vapors. Another alternative is vapor detection using quantum dots [46,47]. In the case of colorimetric indicators, their synthesis is often time-consuming and expensive. Furthermore, there is a lack of solutions that provide all the desired characteristics at the same time.

The obtained materials offer significant advantages in terms of durability, ease of use, and fast response. In addition, this material is quite easy to obtain, when taking into account that opal and dye syntheses are not the most advanced or costly methods. These facts may be particularly important for potential sensing applications. However, further sensitivity tests are still necessary.

This manuscript introduces an innovative and more efficient synthetic pathway for the preparation and spectroscopic characterization of ethyl eosin dye. Our research demonstrates that this dye shows notably high QY. The introduction of the dye soluble in the monomer during the synthesis process does not compromise the quality of the resultant spheres and three-dimensional opals. Notably, the material exhibits angle-dependent emission after the diameter optimization.

Furthermore, our investigations reveal that the QY of the dye-doped spheres is dependent on the dye concentration. This fact demonstrates the potential to obtain a material with a QY similar to that of the dye when dissolved in a solution. Our findings suggest the feasibility of obtaining highly efficient dye-doped polymeric spheres. Noteworthy is the fact that, to the best of our knowledge, this study marks the first instance of absolute QY measurements of dyes embedded in synthesized opals.

Moreover, the obtained PCs exhibit a rapid change in the luminescence properties and color after contact with vapors of inorganic acids commonly used in industry. The unique feature of the EE-doped opals is their selectivity in response to HNO3 vapor. Distribution of the dye molecules in a solid matrix, e.g. in PMMA spheres allows for easier fabrication of indicator systems and much higher QY. These materials can be used to detect acids’ leaks and vapors during storage and transportation as part of a label that can be found on acids. This would increase safety during transportation and usage. Furthermore, the reversibility of such sensor materials can be a great advantage because they can be reused. As a consequence, this fact reduces the cost of using sensors based on these materials. Furthermore, the encapsulation of luminescent sensor dyes in PMMA can be an alternative to the use of aggregation induced emission (AIE) sensor dyes.
This material is available free of charge via the Internet.

W. Z. would like to thank the Ministry of Science and Higher Education in Poland for Grant No. DWD/6/0316/2022. in the frame of Implementation of Doctorate Programme. Part of the work was supported by the National Center for Research and Development (Poland) within the framework of the 12th edition of the LIDER program–project contract no. LIDER/39/0203/L-12/20/NCBR/2021.

Weronika Zając: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Maciej Czajkowski: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Joanna Cybińska: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Dr Sc. Katarzyna Ślepokura from the Faculty of Chemistry at the University of Wrocław for her help in crystallographic analysis of the single crystal structure of the synthesized dye, Dr. Krzysztof Rola from the Łukasiewicz Research Network – PORT Polish Center for Technology Development for execution of the SEM measurements and Dr. Marcin Skoreński from the Łukasiewicz Research Network – PORT Polish Center for Technology Development for dye synthesis.
